info.name: Auth Server - dockerbackend
info.description:
Centralized OAuth2/JWT auth server — covers password login, OTP login, token expiry, login audit, user disable/enable, and cross-service isolation.

---
**Quick Start:**
1. `baseUrl` defaults to `http://localhost:8080`
2. Run any Login request — token auto-saves to `{{currentToken}}`
3. All protected requests pick up `{{currentToken}}` automatically

---
**Pre-seeded users (DataInitializer):**
| User | Password | Service | Roles | Key Permissions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| alice | password123 | service-a | ROLE_ADMIN | READ/WRITE/DELETE_ORDERS |
| bob | password123 | service-a | ROLE_USER | READ_ORDERS only |
| charlie | password123 | service-b | ROLE_ADMIN | READ_REPORTS + READ/MANAGE/DELETE_USERS |
| diana | password123 | service-b | ROLE_USER | READ_REPORTS + READ_USERS only |

---
**Login response shape:**
```json
{
  "accessToken": "<jwt>",
  "tokenType": "Bearer",
  "authMethod": "PASSWORD",
  "expiresAt": "2026-03-19T...",
  "serviceId": "service-a",
  "roles": ["ROLE_ADMIN"],
  "permissions": ["READ_ORDERS", ...]
}
```

**Token error codes (from filter):**
- `TOKEN_EXPIRED` — token signature valid, but past expiry → must re-login
- `INVALID_TOKEN` — signature mismatch / tampered

**Master Admin Key:** `master-secret-key`

variable.description: Auto-populated after any Login request. Used as Bearer token in all protected requests.
Auto-populated by Generate OTP request. Used in OTP login step 2.
Required for register/disable/enable/audit endpoints. Override via MASTER_ADMIN_KEY env var.

item.name: 1. Password Login
item.description: Authenticate with username + password scoped to a service. Every attempt (success or failure) is written to login_audit. Response includes authMethod=PASSWORD and expiresAt.

exec: console.log('[LOGIN] user=alice | authMethod:', json.authMethod, '| roles:', json.roles, '| expiresAt:', json.expiresAt);

request.description: Alice: ROLE_ADMIN in service-a. Permissions: READ_ORDERS, WRITE_ORDERS, DELETE_ORDERS. Token saved to {{currentToken}}.

exec: console.log('[LOGIN] user=bob | authMethod:', json.authMethod, '| roles:', json.roles, '| permissions:', json.permissions);

request.description: Bob: ROLE_USER in service-a. Permissions: READ_ORDERS only. Use Bob's token to test 403 on write/delete/admin endpoints.

exec: console.log('[LOGIN] user=charlie | serviceId:', json.serviceId, '| roles:', json.roles);

request.description: Charlie: ROLE_ADMIN in service-b. Permissions: READ_REPORTS, READ_USERS, MANAGE_USERS, DELETE_USERS.

exec: console.log('[LOGIN] user=diana | roles:', json.roles, '| permissions:', json.permissions);

request.description: Diana: ROLE_USER in service-b. Permissions: READ_REPORTS + READ_USERS only. Use Diana's token to test 403 on delete-user endpoint.

exec: console.log('[AUDIT] This failed login is recorded in login_audit. Check via GET /auth/admin/audit');

request.description: Should return 400. The failure is still recorded in login_audit with success=false and failureReason=Invalid credentials.

exec: console.log('[AUDIT] Cross-service login attempt recorded in audit with serviceId=service-b');

request.description: Alice is not registered in service-b. Returns 400 Invalid credentials.

item.name: 2. OTP Auth Flow
item.description:
Two-step OTP authentication. Generate → Login. The issued JWT carries authMethod=OTP. Both steps write to login_audit.

In production, Step 1 would send OTP via SMS/email and NOT return it in the response. Here it's returned for demo/testing.

exec: console.log('[OTP] Generated:', json.otp, '| Expires at:', json.expiresAt);
console.log('[OTP] Saved to {{currentOtp}} — use in Step 2');

request.description:
Generates a 6-digit OTP. Valid for 5 minutes. Single-use — once consumed by login it cannot be reused. OTP auto-saved to {{currentOtp}} for Step 2.

Response fields: otp, expiresIn (seconds), expiresAt, username, serviceId, note.

exec: console.log('[OTP LOGIN] Success. authMethod:', json.authMethod, '| expiresAt:', json.expiresAt);
console.log('[AUDIT] OTP login recorded in login_audit with authMethod=OTP');

request.description: Verifies the OTP and issues a JWT. The token will have authMethod=OTP in its claims (visible via /api/user/profile). Token saved to {{currentToken}}.

exec: console.log('[AUDIT] Failed OTP attempt recorded in login_audit');

request.description: Generate an OTP first, then submit 000000 instead. Should return 400 Invalid OTP. Failure is written to login_audit.

item.name: OTP Login - Reuse OTP after Step 2 (expect 400 — single use)

exec: console.log('[OTP] OTP is marked used=true after first login — cannot be reused');

request.description: Run Steps 1+2 first, then run this again with the same OTP. The OTP is marked used=true after first login, so this returns 400 — No valid OTP found.

exec: console.log('[OTP] Profile response — note the JWT was issued via OTP, but the claims look identical to password login');
console.log('[OTP] authMethod is embedded in the JWT and visible in login_audit');

request.description: Run after Step 2 OTP login. The profile response is identical to password login — Alice's roles and permissions are the same regardless of auth method.

item.name: 3. Token Validate & Expiry
item.description: Token introspection for other services. The validate endpoint now returns reason=OK, TOKEN_EXPIRED, or INVALID_TOKEN. The filter returns specific error codes directly on protected requests.

exec: console.log('[VALIDATE] valid=true | user:', json.username, '| authMethod:', json.authMethod, '| expires:', json.expiresAt);

request.description:
Login first to populate {{currentToken}}, then call this. Returns: valid, reason=OK, username, userId, serviceId, roles, permissions, authMethod, expiresAt.

Used by downstream services to validate tokens without sharing the JWT secret.

exec: console.log('[VALIDATE] Token expired for user:', json.username, '| expired at:', json.expiresAt);

request.description:
Paste an expired JWT here. The validate endpoint returns valid=false, reason=TOKEN_EXPIRED, and still returns the claims (username, serviceId, roles) so the caller knows whose token it was.

To get a real expired token: set jwt.expiration=1 in application.properties, login, wait 1ms, then validate.
A JWT with bad/mismatched signature. Returns valid=false, reason=INVALID_TOKEN.

item.name: Expired Token on Protected Endpoint (expect 401 TOKEN_EXPIRED)

exec: console.log('[FILTER] Token expired — filter returned 401 with code=TOKEN_EXPIRED before request reached controller');

request.description:
Replace the Authorization header with an expired token. The JwtAuthenticationFilter intercepts this BEFORE the request reaches the controller and returns:

```json
{ "error": "Your token has expired. Please log in again.", "code": "TOKEN_EXPIRED" }
```

This is different from INVALID_TOKEN — the client knows to re-authenticate vs investigate a tampered token.

item.name: Tampered Token on Protected Endpoint (expect 401 INVALID_TOKEN)

request.description: Sends a JWT with a bad signature. Filter returns 401 with code=INVALID_TOKEN.

item.name: 4. Public (No Auth)
item.description: Accessible without any token.

request.description: No Authorization header needed. Open to all.

item.name: 5. User Profile (Any Authenticated)
item.description: Requires any valid JWT. Profile response includes roles, permissions and serviceId extracted from the token.

exec: console.log('[PROFILE] user:', json.username, '| service:', json.serviceId, '| roles:', json.roles);

request.description: Returns: username, userId, serviceId, roles, permissions — all parsed from the JWT by the PermissionInterceptor. Works with any logged-in user (password or OTP).
No token sent. Spring Security rejects unauthenticated access to /api/**.

item.name: 6. Admin Endpoints (ROLE_ADMIN)
item.description: Requires ROLE_ADMIN in JWT. Alice (service-a) and Charlie (service-b) qualify. Bob and Diana do not.

request.description: Login as Alice or Charlie first. @PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')") enforced by Spring Security method security.
Login as Bob first. Bob has ROLE_USER only. Spring Security's @PreAuthorize denies and returns 403.

item.name: 7. Service-A — Orders
item.description:
Permission-gated endpoints via @RequiresPermission.
alice: READ + WRITE + DELETE_ORDERS
bob: READ_ORDERS only

request.description: @RequiresPermission("READ_ORDERS"). Both alice and bob qualify.
@RequiresPermission("WRITE_ORDERS"). Login as Alice: 200. Login as Bob: 403.
Login as Bob first. Bob lacks WRITE_ORDERS. PermissionInterceptor returns 403 with the missing permission named.
@RequiresPermission("DELETE_ORDERS"). Login as Alice: 200. Login as Bob: 403.

item.name: 8. Service-B — Reports & Users
item.description:
Permission-gated endpoints for service-b.
charlie: READ_REPORTS + READ/MANAGE/DELETE_USERS
diana: READ_REPORTS + READ_USERS only

request.description: @RequiresPermission("READ_REPORTS"). Both charlie and diana qualify.

item.name: GET Users (READ_USERS OR MANAGE_USERS) — Charlie or Diana: 200

request.description: @RequiresPermission(value={"READ_USERS","MANAGE_USERS"}, requireAll=false). Either permission is sufficient. Both charlie and diana qualify.
@RequiresPermission(value={"MANAGE_USERS","DELETE_USERS"}, requireAll=true). BOTH required. Login as Charlie: 200.

item.name: DELETE User — Diana: 403 (has READ_USERS but not MANAGE+DELETE)

request.description: Login as Diana first. Diana lacks MANAGE_USERS and DELETE_USERS. requireAll=true means both are needed. Returns 403.

item.name: 9. Disable / Enable User
item.description: Admin operations. Disabled users cannot log in — new attempts return 403. Existing tokens remain valid until expiry (JWT is stateless — to force-invalidate, reduce jwt.expiration or implement a token blacklist).

exec: console.log('[ADMIN] Bob disabled. His next login attempt will return 403.');
console.log('[AUDIT] Disabled login attempt recorded in login_audit with success=false, failureReason=User account is disabled');

request.description: Run after Disable Bob. Returns 403 User account is disabled. The failure is still written to login_audit.

exec: console.log('[ADMIN] Bob re-enabled. Login will work again.');

item.name: 10. Login Audit
item.description: Only login events are recorded — never regular API requests. Each record contains: username, serviceId, authMethod (PASSWORD/OTP), success, failureReason, ipAddress, loginAt, tokenExpiresAt.

exec: console.log('[AUDIT] Last login | user:', json.username, '| authMethod:', json.authMethod, '| at:', json.loginAt, '| ip:', json.ipAddress);

request.description: Most recent successful login for alice in service-a. Shows loginAt, ipAddress, authMethod, and tokenExpiresAt.

item.name: Full Audit Log — All events in service-a

exec: console.log('[AUDIT] Total events in service-a:', json.totalElements, '| showing page:', json.number);
json.content.forEach(e => console.log(' -', e.username, '|', e.authMethod, '|', e.success ? 'SUCCESS' : 'FAIL: ' + e.failureReason, '|', e.loginAt));

request.description: Paginated audit log for all users in service-a, newest first. Run several logins (success + failure + OTP) first to populate data.
Filtered to Alice's history only. You should see both PASSWORD and OTP authMethod entries if you've used both flows.
All login events for service-b. Each service has its own isolated audit view.

item.name: 11. Service Registration
item.description: Dynamically onboard new services and users. Requires X-Admin-Key.

request.description: Registers a new service. After this, create roles/permissions for it (via DataInitializer or DB), then register users.
Registers a user with specific roles and permissions. Roles/permissions must already exist for the service. Passwords are bcrypt-hashed at registration.

item.name: 12. Cross-Service Isolation
item.description: A token issued for service-a cannot access service-b endpoints and vice versa. The PermissionInterceptor reads permissions from the JWT — cross-service permissions are never present in the token.

exec: console.log('[ISOLATION] Alice service-a token has no READ_REPORTS — that permission only exists in service-b');

request.description: Login as Alice (service-a) first. Her JWT contains only service-a permissions. READ_REPORTS is a service-b permission — not embedded in the token.

exec: console.log('[ISOLATION] Charlie service-b token has no READ_ORDERS — that permission only exists in service-a');

request.description: Login as Charlie (service-b) first. His JWT contains only service-b permissions. READ_ORDERS is a service-a permission.

item.name: 8. Document Generation
item.description:
PDF generation via Playwright (Chrome engine). No auth required — endpoints are under /api/public/**.

Responds with Content-Type: application/pdf and triggers a file download.

The browser pool warms up at startup (~1-2 s first time), then each PDF takes ~150-300 ms.

**Available types:** INVOICE, REPORT

request.description:
Downloads a dummy invoice PDF generated by Chrome (Playwright).

No token required — public endpoint.

In Postman: click **Send and Download** (dropdown on Send button) to save the .pdf file.

Downloads a dummy activity report PDF generated by Chrome (Playwright).

No token required — public endpoint.
Passes an unknown DocType enum value. Spring should reject with 400 Bad Request (MethodArgumentTypeMismatchException).

Downloads a dummy invoice PDF generated by Chrome (Playwright).

No token required — public endpoint.

In Postman: click **Send and Download** (dropdown on Send button) to save the .pdf file.

Downloads a dummy activity report PDF generated by Chrome (Playwright).

No token required — public endpoint.
Passes an unknown DocType enum value. Spring should reject with 400 Bad Request (MethodArgumentTypeMismatchException).
